items.md: The supporting characters are humanoid dogs; Barks said in the 1971 interview that he would have preferred to have drawn them as real humans, but for the warning he had been given when he submitted "Dangerous Disguise" for Donald Duck Four Color No. 308, 1951. (see the note to that story).
items.value: The supporting characters are humanoid dogs; Barks said in the 1971 interview that he would have preferred to have drawn them as real humans, but for the warning he had been given when he submitted "Dangerous Disguise" for Donald Duck Four Color No. 308, 1951. (see the note to that story).

items.md: Front cover (art only): Donald uses a magnifying glass to inspect a hungry crocodile's hide. (Illustrating "The Crocodile Collector.") (Apr. 5, 1951)

items.md: ## Donald Duck in A Christmas for Shacktown
items.value: Donald Duck in A Christmas for Shacktown

items.md: Front cover: Donald, dressed as Santa, rides a toy train, while the nephews labor under a heavy sack. (Illustrating "A Christmas for Shacktown.") (May 24, 1951)

items.md: DONALD DUCK - 1 (inside front cover) - art only - The nephews make tree ornaments out of Donald's golf balls. Reprinted: Christmas Parade (Gold Key) No. 2, 1964.

items.caption: Barks holds his own copy of Donald Duck Four Color No. 189, 1948, the first comic book for which he illustrated the cover. The words stamped on the cover read as follows: "This comic is the personal file copy of Carl Barks. Anyone else possessing the book has stolen it." Comic-book cover © 1948 Walt Disney Productions.

Barks holds his own copy of *Donald Duck* Four Color No. 189, 1948, the first comic book for which he illustrated the cover. The words stamped on the cover read as follows: "This comic is the personal file copy of Carl Barks. Anyone else possessing the book has stolen it." Comic-book cover © 1948 Walt Disney Productions.
items.value: Barks holds his own copy of Donald Duck Four Color No. 189, 1948, the first comic book for which he illustrated the cover.
